story:
While picking fruit for a summer pudding, Alice hears a thin, wandering melody drift across her garden. At the front gate stands a down-at-heel traveler with a strange stringed instrument tucked under his arm, a small crank jutting from its side. He asks for water. She leads him to the garden bench, brings him a cup and a slice of cake, and learns his name—Tom Buckland. The contraption, he says, is a wheel fiddle, some call it a hurdy-gurdy. He earns a few coins with it. “You can sing for your supper then,” she says, half in jest.

When he has finished eating, she asks to see the instrument. It is scratched and chipped, but the old ivory and brass hint that it was once handsome. She strokes its curved body, then gives it back. He settles it on his lap, turns the crank, and presses the keys. A wavering, wailing tune spills out. He sings in a higher voice than she expects, in a language she does not know. Gooseflesh rises on her arms, the fine hairs at her nape lift, and a shiver spreads up her back and over her scalp. The melody seems to move the air around them. His dark eyes do not leave her. Her knees loosen; she sinks onto the lawn and lies back. Overhead, swifts and martins cleave the blue, turning and racing. She closes her eyes.

She wakes with a start at the sound of the garden gate. Her husband comes in, and the light tells her time has passed. The traveler has gone. Cup and plate sit neatly stacked on the bench. “I thought I heard music,” her husband says. Alice stands, smooths her dress. “Music?” she answers.

The baby is born in March, right in lambing time—never a good season for a shepherd’s wife to be delivered of a child. Her mother always said there is no good time. A careful shepherd keeps his distance in June, and her husband had done so. He does not speak of dates. June is far away, and memories blur. The boy, when he grows, has dark eyes and a brown complexion. Alice names him Martin, for the birds that nested under the eaves last summer.

By ten, Martin runs errands to the village and lives the small adventures of a country boy. One day he hears a tune around a bend and meets a shabby man walking and turning the handle of a wheel fiddle. The man stops, looks at him closely. “Would you like to try, Martin? Alice named you for the martins that nested under the eaves that summer.” They sit on the bank. The stranger shows him how to press the keys and keep the crank moving. “You’ve a fair talent for it,” he says. “Keep it. I’ve no use for it no more.” He rises and walks on. The boy hardly notices him go. The eerie music seems to play itself as he wanders home.

Alice meets him at the door, face drained. “Where did you get that thing?” “A travelling man gave it to me,” Martin says. “He knew my name. He said you named me for the martins under the eaves.” The tune catches at her again, though the pull is weaker now. The hairs on her neck stir. “We’ll see what your father says,” she begins, then softens, knowing the boy’s father has already had his say.
